name: Under Armour, Inc.

profile.description: Under Armour, Inc. designs, develops, markets, and distributes performance apparel, footwear, and accessories for men, women, and youth globally. The company sells through wholesale channels (sporting goods chains, department stores, specialty retailers) and direct-to-consumer via brand and factory house stores and e-commerce. Under new CEO Kevin Plank (returned 2024), UAA is executing a multi-year brand turnaround focused on product innovation and premium positioning. Headquartered in Baltimore, Maryland.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (1/3): Under Armour has been executing a defensive brand repositioning — exiting low-margin wholesale channels and reducing inventory — rather than launching transformative new products. FY2026 revenue declined 3.8% GAAP with North America down 7.9% [1]. CEO Plank described the 'most disruptive' phase of the turnaround as over, but no breakthrough product launches have materialized. AI Deployment (0/2): Under Armour has a data analytics team exploring AI for merchandising and customer personalization, but no AI products are shipping to customers and no quantified AI-driven revenue or cost savings were reported [2]. Market Share (1/2): UAA maintains a meaningful position in premium performance athletic wear (behind Nike and Adidas) with some strength in team sports, but market share has been declining in North America for several years. Product Pipeline (1/2): FY2027 guidance projects modest revenue declines with a focus on 'transformative innovations' in product, but no specific next-gen products were named with launch timelines [1]. R&D Intensity (1/1): Under Armour invests meaningfully in materials science and performance technology relative to apparel peers.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): Under Armour's mission — 'to make all athletes better through passion, design, and the relentless pursuit of innovation' — is specific, distinctive, and clearly connected to the brand's founding identity. It differentiates from Nike's aspirational marketing and Adidas's lifestyle positioning. Kevin Plank's return represents an effort to re-anchor the company in this original purpose. Community Impact (1/2): Under Armour maintains athlete partnerships and community sports programs, including youth athletic development initiatives, though no distinctive large-scale charitable programs with specific dollar amounts were identified. Employee Retention (0/2): Multiple waves of restructuring, CEO transitions (Plank to Frisk to Plank), and workforce reductions from 16,000+ to 6,200 employees indicate significant organizational instability [2]. Glassdoor rating of 3.7/5 is below average for major consumer brands. Insider Buying (1/2): The $500M share buyback program signals management conviction in the turnaround, and Plank maintains a substantial equity stake as founder, though this is ownership rather than open-market buying.

infrastructure.label: WEAK
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored as a growth stock. FY2026 (ending March 2026) delivered $4.97B revenue (-3.8% GAAP) with a net loss of $496M and negative ROE of -30.0% [1]. D/E of 137.1% is elevated with $1.94B total debt against $309M cash. Profit margin of -10.0% reflects ongoing restructuring charges and brand investment costs. Revenue growth 5yr CAGR of -5.6% demonstrates sustained contraction. Forward P/E of 24.7 is expensive for a loss-making company recovering toward profitability. PEG ratio of 1.84 provides modest valuation support if earnings recovery materializes. Free cash flow is deeply negative at -$528M, a serious concern for a company with limited liquidity. The only metric scoring well is D/E (below the 300% floor) and forward P/E (below the 60 floor) [4].

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (6/9): Under Armour's core business — designing, manufacturing, and marketing physical athletic apparel and footwear — involves tangible products and brand identity that AI cannot substitute [1]. Athletic wear requires physical fit, material performance, and brand loyalty that software cannot replace. However, AI poses real competitive threats: Nike's 190M+ app users generate superior data for AI-driven personalization, and generative AI tools could accelerate product design cycles at competitors, widening the innovation gap. AI could also automate portions of Under Armour's merchandising and supply chain optimization. AI Opportunity (1/1): Under Armour's Connected Fitness platform (MapMyFitness) and data analytics team generate modest AI-related value through personalized recommendations and demand forecasting, though this is operational rather than a revenue driver [2].

infinite_game.summary: Under Armour's return of founder Kevin Plank as CEO represents an attempt to reconnect the company with its founding athletic performance identity — a potentially infinite game restoration. However, the company has been in reactive restructuring for five years, workforce has shrunk from 16,000+ to 6,200, and the brand's aspirational positioning has been eroded by years of over-expansion into off-price channels. Sinek would recognize the Just Cause but question whether the organizational foundation remains strong enough to sustain it.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): Under Armour's founding purpose — making athletes better through performance innovation — is a genuine, specific cause that Plank articulated clearly and that differentiated the brand for its first 20 years. The return of Plank represents an attempt to restore this cause after years of strategic drift under prior CEO Patrik Frisk. The mission is distinctive and clearly lived at the founding, but has not yet been re-validated through consistent execution in this recovery phase.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): Multiple waves of restructuring, CEO changes (Plank to Frisk to Plank), executive leadership changes announced in May 2026 [2], and workforce reductions from 16,000+ to 6,200 indicate a low-trust, unstable organizational environment. Glassdoor rating of 3.7/5 with only 3.0/5 for career opportunities suggests limited employee confidence. Plank's stated belief in micromanagement ('at certain levels') creates tension with Sinek's Circle of Safety concept [2].

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): Under Armour explicitly positions against Nike and Adidas as worthy rivals whose strengths in brand storytelling, global distribution, and digital ecosystems have exposed UA's weaknesses. Plank's return narrative is explicitly about learning from 'what Nike did right' and using competitive pressure to improve UA's own product and brand execution [2].

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): Under Armour has restructured significantly — exiting unprofitable wholesale channels, reducing headcount, and refocusing on premium direct-to-consumer positioning. However, these were defensive retrenchments forced by declining performance rather than proactive strategic pivots. The brand is returning to its roots rather than innovating into new markets — necessary but not transformative in Sinek's framework.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Plank's willingness to accept ongoing revenue decline (-3.8% FY2026, modest declines guided FY2027) in service of rebuilding brand equity and margins demonstrates some courageous long-term thinking over short-term revenue defense [1]. Accepting $496M in net losses while investing in brand repositioning requires conviction. However, the restructuring was largely forced by competitive pressure rather than voluntary sacrifice.

investment_thesis: Under Armour's bull case is a founder-led turnaround at deeply depressed valuations: at $5.99/share and $2.55B market cap, UAA trades near tangible book value with significant option value if Plank's brand repositioning succeeds. Gross margin of ~48% demonstrates underlying pricing power in the performance athletic segment. The $500M buyback program signals management conviction. EMEA showed positive growth in FY2026, and FY2027 guidance suggests the 'most disruptive' phase of restructuring is behind the company [1][2]. If North American revenue stabilizes and profit margins turn positive, the stock could re-rate significantly from current depressed levels.
thesis_continued: The bear case is severe: FY2026 delivered a $496M net loss with -30% ROE, negative free cash flow of $528M, and revenue declining for the third consecutive year to $4.97B [1]. D/E of 137% with $1.94B debt against $309M cash raises going-concern risk if the turnaround stalls. FY2027 guidance projects continued modest revenue declines, meaning UAA could be four consecutive years into revenue contraction before any growth materializes. Nike and Lululemon continue to take share with superior digital ecosystems and brand execution. Workforce reduction from 16,000+ to 6,200 may have stripped the organization of the talent needed to execute a successful turnaround. The apparel industry is highly competitive, and brand comebacks of this magnitude are historically rare [2].
key_catalysts: North American revenue stabilization and return to growth in FY2028, demonstrating that the premium brand repositioning and wholesale channel cleanup are generating demand recovery

GAAP profitability recovery — FY2027 adjusted EPS guidance of $0.10 at midpoint would represent a crucial inflection from the -$1.16 trailing EPS, though management's own guidance missed estimates by 55.9%

Successful footwear product launch capturing meaningful market share — footwear is Under Armour's weakest category relative to Nike and Adidas, and a breakthrough would significantly expand the addressable market
key_risks: Nike and Adidas accelerating AI-driven personalization and digital ecosystem advantages that systematically erode Under Armour's remaining performance positioning among data-savvy athletes
Tariff escalation on Asian-sourced manufacturing increasing COGS and compressing the ~48% gross margin that is currently the primary financial bright spot in the turnaround

Kevin Plank departure or leadership instability — the bull case requires founder-led brand restoration over 3-5 years; the May 2026 executive leadership changes signal continued organizational flux that could undermine execution continuity
sources: Under Armour FY2026 Full-Year and Q4 Results — SEC 8-K and 10-K, May 2026
Under Armour CEO Kevin Plank Leadership, Glassdoor Reviews, Executive Changes — Retail Dive, Fortune, May 2026
Under Armour ESG Ratings and Sustainability Assessment — KnowESG, Good On You, accessed May 2026
Yahoo Finance — UAA Key Statistics and Financial Data, accessed May 2026
